coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor for copper ions and cadmium ions in scallops based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots cooperating with gold nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xue
Ma, Zhiying
Sun, Minjun
Liu, Xiuying
Zhong, Keli
Tang, Lijun
Li, Xuepeng
Li, Jianrong
coredata.dc:description: Based on the electrostatic interaction, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescence nanomixture of graphene quantum dots-gold nanoclusters (GQDs-AuNCs) for the quantitative detection of Cu2+ and Cd2+. When Cu2+ or Cd2+ was added into the reaction system, the fluorescence of GSH-AuNCs at 565 nm can be quenched by Cu2+ and enhanced by Cd2+ while the intensity of N-GQDs at 403 nm stayed constant. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I565/I403) of the GQDs-AuNCs system was proportional to the concentration of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in the range of 8×10−8 mol/L-6×10−6 mol/L and 1×10−6 mol/L-4×10−5 mol/L, respectively, with detection limits of 4.12×10−9 mol/L and 9.43×10−7 mol/L, respectively. In the presence of Cu2+ and Cd2+, the paper-based vision sensor would produce visible fluorescent color changes, which can be used for rapid detection on site. The method has been successfully applied to the determination of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallops with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs-AuNCs
Cu2+
Cd2+
Ratiometric fluorescence
Paper-based sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cu2+ or Cd2+ based on GQDs-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe
Detection of Cu2+ or Cd2+ in actual samples
Characterization of N-GQDs, AuNCs and GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture
Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ and Cd2+
Study on the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
Visual detection of Cu2+ and Cd2+
Detection of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallops

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-AuNCs in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ (A-G: 8×10−8 mol/L, 2×10−7 mol/L, 4×10−7 mol/L, 2×10−6 mol/L, 4×10−6 mol/L, 6×10−6 mol/L). (b) Plots of fluorescence intensity ratio of (I565/I403)/(I565/I403)0 versus the concentration of Cu2+. The illustration shows the fluorescence color changes of the reaction system in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ (0, 0.6 μmol/L, 0.8 μmol/L, 2 μmol/L, 4 μmol/L, 6 μmol/L) under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-AuNCs in the presence of different concentrations of Cd2+ (A-H: 1×10−6 mol/L, 2×10−6 mol/L, 4×10−6 mol/L, 6×10−6 mol/L, 2×10−5 mol/L, 3×10−5 mol/L, 4×10−5 mol/L). (b) Plots of fluorescence intensity ratio of (I565/I403)/(I565/I403)0 versus the concentration of Cd2+. The illustration shows the fluorescence color changes of the reaction system in the presence of different concentrations of Cd2+ (0, 6 μmol/L, 8 μmol/L, 10 μmol/L, 20 μmol/L, 40 μmol/L) under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) The fluorescence intensity of the reaction system changes in the presence of different ions. (0.2 μmol/L Cu2+, 30 μmol/L Cd2+, 0.2 mmol/L Pb2+, 4 mmol/L Zn2+, 4 mmol/L Cr3+, 4 mmol/L Fe3+, 4 mmol/L Fe2+, 4 mmol/L Mg2+, 4 mmol/L K+, 4 mmol/L Mn2+, 4 mmol/L Ba2+, 4 mmol/L Cl−, 4 mmol/L I−, 4 mmol/L SO4 2−, 4 mmol/L CO3 2–, 4 mmol/L F−). (b) The fluorescence intensity changes of the reaction system in the presence of copper and other ions (The concentration of each ion is the same as above.). (c) The fluorescence intensity changes of the reaction system in the presence of cadmium and other ions (The concentration of each ion is the same as above.).

(a) Color change of paper-based sensor in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+. (b) Color change of paper-based sensor in the presence of different concentrations of Cd2+. (c) Color change of paper-based sensor after adding standard substance to actual sample. They were irradiated with 365 nm ultraviolet lamp.
Mechanism diagram of GQDs-AuNCs for the detection of Cu2+ and Cd2+.
Determination of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallops by standard addition recovery.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor for copper ions and cadmium ions in scallops based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots cooperating with gold nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University, National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology
National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products
Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province
The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University, National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Bohai University, Jinzhou, Liaoning 121013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Bohai University, Jinzhou, Liaoning 121013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on the electrostatic interaction, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescence nanomixture of graphene quantum dots-gold nanoclusters (GQDs-AuNCs) for the quantitative detection of Cu2+ and Cd2+. When Cu2+ or Cd2+ was added into the reaction system, the fluorescence of GSH-AuNCs at 565 nm can be quenched by Cu2+ and enhanced by Cd2+ while the intensity of N-GQDs at 403 nm stayed constant. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I565/I403) of the GQDs-AuNCs system was proportional to the concentration of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in the range of 8×10−8 mol/L-6×10−6 mol/L and 1×10−6 mol/L-4×10−5 mol/L, respectively, with detection limits of 4.12×10−9 mol/L and 9.43×10−7 mol/L, respectively. In the presence of Cu2+ and Cd2+, the paper-based vision sensor would produce visible fluorescent color changes, which can be used for rapid detection on site. The method has been successfully applied to the determination of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallops with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of world industrialization, heavy metal pollution has seriously affected all parts of the environment, including life on land and in water (Bansod, Kumar, Thakur, Rana, & Singh, 2017). The common heavy metals in aquatic products are Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cr6+ and so on. These heavy metals are usually highly toxic and can enter people's bodies through the food chain to threaten people's health. Cu2+ is an essential trace element for human body and plays a key role in many physiological balances of human body. However, if Cu2+ is excessive, it will lead to oxidative stress and disturb intracellular homeostasis, causing a series of diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, Menkes, Wilson diseases and kidney damage (Kumar, et al., 2018; Liu, Jia, Wang, Wang, Gao, & Ren, 2019). Cd2+ is another common heavy metal pollutant. Cd2+ cannot be naturally degraded and easily accumulated in human body (Pan, Wang, Guo, Liu, Tang, & Zhang, 2018; Mohanasundaram, Bhaskar, Kumar, Rajesh, & Rajagopal, 2021). Excessive Cd2+ in human body will lead to a series of diseases such as renal insufficiency and cancer. Therefore, it is very important to develop a rapid and reliable detection technology for Cu2+ and Cd2+. The detection methods of metal ion have been developed, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (Zhang, Dong, & Xu, 2016), atomic absorption spectrometry (Kandhro, Soylak, Kazi, Yilmaz, & Afridi, 2012), capillary electrophoresis (Popova, Sursyakova, Burmakina, & Rubaylo, 2015), voltammetry (Duay, Ortiz-Santiago, & Lambert, 2017) and so on. However, these methods require a large number of samples, expensive instruments and complexed operation procedures (Rao et al., 2016). In addition, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy is often used to detect heavy metals because of its simple sampling, fast data acquisition and noninvasive (Guo, Chen, Elseedi, Zou, & Yang, 2021). However, this method is easily affected by fluorescence phenomenon, optical system parameters and so on. Fluorescent sensors have attracted much attention because of their high sensitivity (Zhong et al., 2020), easy to use and cheap equipment (Liu, Jia, Wang, Wang, Gao, & Ren, 2019). By measuring the ratio of photoluminescence (PL) intensity of two wavelengths to detect the concentration of the substance, the ratiometric fluorescent probe has the functions of insensitive to background and instrument, built-in correction, eliminating the effect of excitation and emission efficiency, environmental impact and the influence of probe concentration (Rao et al., 2016; Zeng, Zhang, Zhu, Jia, Li, & Xue, 2011; Yan, Li, Li, & Su, 2014; Wu et al., 2015). Therefore, compared with single emission fluorescent, the ratiometric fluorescence probe has higher sensitivity.

In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention due to superior optical properties, long fluorescence lifetime, good photostability, good resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, and stable emission (Shi et al., 2015). In addition, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are also the most studied fluorescent nanomaterials. AuNCs show stronger catalytic activity, luminescence and unique charging characteristics owing to the space limitation of free electrons. These optical properties of GQDs and AuNCs make them superior to organic dyes containing toxic metal ions and semiconductor quantum dots which are prone to photobleaching (Yan et al., 2016). In 2015, Lin et al. prepared N-GQDs with strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation using citric acid and trihydroxymethyl aminomethane as raw materials for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (Lin, et al., 2015). In 2017, Liu et al. established a simple and sensitive method for the determination of alkaline phosphatase based on the internal filtering effect of p-nitrophenylphosphate on the fluorescence of AuNCs (Liu, Li, Xia, & Ren, 2017). However, one emission peak is easy to be affected by the concentration of probe and external interference and the sensitivity is relatively low. The ratiometric fluorescence probe can overcome this problem. Furthermore, the fluorescence color of the probe can change with the change of the concentration of the substance under UV irradiation. Visual inspection can be carried out according to the color change (Liu et al., 2018). Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was prepared to detect Cu2+ and Cd2+.

In this work, we prepared a ratiometric fluorescent probe to detect Cu2+ and Cd2+ based on the dual-emission fluorescent nanomixture (GQDs-AuNCs) (Scheme 1 ). The novel nanomixture exhibited two strong emission peaks at 403 nm and 565 nm under 320 nm excitation, and showed pink fluorescence under UV light. Due to the charge transfer mechanism and the formation of core-shell heterostructures, the fluorescence of GSH-AuNCs is quenched by Cu2+ and enhanced by Cd2+. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of N-GQDs is not affected. The experimental results show that the sensor has the advantages of short reaction time, high sensitivity and good selectivity. The method has been successfully applied to the determination of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallops.

Graphite powder (99.5%), copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2‧3H2O), Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4‧3H2O) and phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5, ≥98%) were obtained from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Potassium persulfate (K2O8S2) was obtained from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Plant (Tianjin, China). Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) was obtained from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Reduced glutathione (GSH) was obtained from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4‧12H2O) was obtained from Tianjin FengChuan chemical reagent Technology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O) was obtained from Sinopharm group (Guangdong, China). All the reagents used in the experiment were analytically pure. Deionized water was used in all experiment.

The fluorescence spectrum and relative fluorescence intensity were recorded by Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5 nm and 10 nm respectively. The excitation wavelength was 320 nm and the spectrum measurement range was 375–620 nm. The infrared spectra of GQDs-AuNCs was recorded by IR tracer-100 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer of Shimadzu company. The morphology of N-GQDs and GSH-AuNCs were observed by Tecnai G2 F20/F30 field emission transmission electron microscope. The particle size of GSH-AuNCs and Zeta potentials of N-GQDs, GSH-AuNCs and GQDs-AuNCs were measured by Marvin Nano-ZS90 laser particle size analyzer. Study on the chemical states of GQDs-AuNCs by Thermo ESCALAB 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Shimadzu ultraviolet spectrophotometer was used to determine the ultraviolet absorption spectrum with deionized water as reference solution. The S2020-M60-B-23 shaker made by CY-TECH Co., Ltd. was used to disperse the liquid evenly. The pH value of phosphate buffer solution was adjusted by PHS-3C pH meter of BSNTE Co., Ltd. The content of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallops was determined by Agilent ICPOES730 of America to verify the accuracy of the method.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared using the Hummers’ method with slightly modification as previously reported (Gao, Zhang, Zhang, Tang, Liu, & Li, 2018). N-GQDs were further prepared using GO through one-step solvothermal method. First, 0.3 g GO was added into 9 mL ammonia water (28%) and 15 mL deionized water, and stirred for 1.5 h at room temperature. Then, the obtained suspension was introduced into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)(Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL). After reacting at 170 °C for 6 h, the obtained suspension was cooled to room temperature and was filtered through a 0.22 μM membrane. The supernatant was condensed at 100 ℃ for 1.5 h to obtain N-GQDs. N-GQDs was stored at room temperature.

The GSH-capping AuNCs were synthesized according to the procedure from a previously reported paper (Wang et al., 2019). First, 2 mL 10 mM HAuCl4, 3 mL 10 mM GSH and 10 mL ultrapure water were mixed and stirred for 10 min. Then the mixture was heated and stirred at 70 °C for 24 h. Finally, the heat source was removed and the resulting liquid was centrifuged at 9000 rpm/min for 5 min to remove impurities. GSH-AuNCs was obtained and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ or Cd2+ based on GQDs-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared N-GQDs were diluted 10 times. Then, the 5 mL diluted N-GQDs and 5 mL GSH-AuNCs were simply mixed to obtain GQDs-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe. The probe was stored at 4 °C for further use. 200 μL GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture and different concentrations of Cu2+ or Cd2+ were put into a 2 mL centrifuge tubes. Then, phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7) was added to 1.5 mL. The fluorescence intensity was measured immediately. The working curves were also plotted between the values of the intensity ratio (I565/I403)/(I565/I403)0 (I565 and I403 were the fluorescence intensity of GSH-AuNCs and N-GQDs, respectively. (I565/I403)0 and (I565/I403) were the fluorescence intensity ratio before and after adding the tested substance, respectively.) and the concentrations of Cu2+ or Cd2+.

200 μL GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture and the interfering ions (Cu2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, K+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Cl−, I−, SO4 2−, CO3 2– and F−) were put into a 2 mL centrifuge tube. Then, phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7) was added to 1.5 mL and measured the fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ or Cd2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Because there were many nutrients in food, it may interfere with the test results during the test (Yu et al., 2019). Therefore, the scallop samples should be pretreated before the test. Scallop samples were obtained from the local market. Five grams shredded scallops and 4 mL of 0.7 mol/L dilute nitric acid were soaked in 15 mL the crucible for 12 h. The obtained suspension was filtered with 0.22 μmol/L membrane to obtain supernatant. The pH of the supernatant was adjusted to 7 with 2 mol/L NaOH and the volume was fixed to 10 mL with pH = 7 buffer solution. The obtained liquid was used for the next determination.

Take part of the above solution, the Cu2+ and Cd2+ in the samples were detected by ICP-OES. At the same time, take two parts of the solution and add the standard substance to it for the standard addition of ICP-OES.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-GQDs, AuNCs and GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1a and Fig. S1b showed TEM images of the prepared N-GQDs and GSH-AuNCs, respectively, indicating that they were spherical and well dispersed in the solution. The illustrations of Fig. S1a and Fig S1b showed the particle size distribution of N-GQDs and GSH-AuNCs in the range of 1.30–3.32 nm and 2.01–3.62 nm with an average particle size of 2.26 nm and 2.78 nm, respectively. Zeta potentials of N-GQDs, GSH-AuNCs and GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture were shown in Fig. S2. The Zeta potential of N-GQDs was + 2.47 ± 0.34 mV due to the presence of many amino groups on the surface. The Zeta potential of GSH-AuNCs was −29.90 ± 2.79 mV, which was attributed to the fact that GSH contains two carboxyl groups and an amino group. Due to the opposite charge on the surface of N-GQDs and GSH-AuNCs, a charge-charge interaction occurred between them after assembly. The zeta potential of GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture was observed at −9.41 ± 2.76 mV. Therefore, N-GQDs and GSH-AuNCs were assembled by electrostatic interaction (Wang et al., 2019).

In order to study the chemical states, we measured the XPS spectrum and FTIR spectrum of GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture (Fig. S3). The XPS spectrum of the GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture (Fig. S3e) showed four peaks of about 83.97, 284.97, 399.97 and 530.97 eV, corresponding to Au4f, C1s, N1s and O1s elements, respectively. The deconvolution spectra of C 1 s (Fig. S3a) included a CC/CC peak with a binding energy of 284.8 eV, a C-OH peak of 286.0 eV and a CO peak of 288.1 eV (Li, et al., 2016; Liu, Jia, Zhang, Tang, Wang, & Fang, 2020). The deconvolution spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. S3b) consisted of a CNC peak with a binding energy of 399.7 eV and a NH peak of 401.1 eV (Zhao, Song, Chai, Zhao, He, & Liu, 2020). The deconvolution spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. S3c) consisted of a CO peak with a binding energy of 530.8 eV and a C-OH/COC peak of 531.5 eV (Ju, & Chen, 2015; Qu et al., 2015; Zhang, et al., 2015). In the high-resolution region of Au 4f (Fig. S3d), two binding energies (84.2 and 88.0 eV) were detected, which belong to 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 respectively. It was like the previous reports (Demoisson, Raes, Terryn, Guillot, Migeon, & Reniers, 2008; Zhang et al., 2015). The binding energy spectrum of Au 4f7/2 could be deconvoluted into two peaks, with Au(0) at 83.9 eV and Au(I) at 84.3 eV, respectively (Shamsipur, Molaabasi, Shanehsaz, & Moosavi-Movahedi, 2014). The peaks at 1048, 1123, 1400, 1618, 3127 and 3451 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture (Fig. S3f) were attributed to S-Au, COC, CN, CO, NH and OH, respectively (Liu et al., 2018; Arcudi, Dordevic, & Prato, 2016). The results of FTIR and XPS were consistent.

UV absorption spectrogram (Fig. S4a) showed the obvious absorption peak of N-GQDs at 298 nm was the electron transition from n-orbital to π* orbital of surface oxygen groups (Qu et al., 2015; Zhang, et al., 2015). GSH-AuNCs nanomixture had an absorption peak at about 400 nm, which was consistent with the previous reports (Zhang, Wu, Liu, Gu, & Chen, 2014). The UV absorption spectra of GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture had obvious peaks at 298 nm and 400 nm. As shown in Fig. S4b, at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm, the emission peak of N-GQDs was at 403 nm and GSH-AuNCs was at 565 nm. After assembly, GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture had both emission peaks at 403 nm and 565 nm. Compared with the emission peaks of N-GQDs and GSH-AuNCs, the emission peak intensity of GQDs-AuNCs nanomixture at 403 nm decreased and increased at 565 nm. This phenomenon might be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between N-GQDs and GSH-AuNCs. The fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-AuNCs and UV absorption spectra of N-GQDs (Fig. S4c) further confirmed the existence of FRET. The quantum yields of GQDsAuNCs was 24.42% using quinine sulfate (In 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, the quantum yield was 54.6% when the excitation wavelength was 360 nm.) as the reference material.

As shown in Fig. S5a, the fluorescence quenching of GSH-AuNCs by Cu2+ was more obvious under neutral and alkaline conditions. Because the carboxyl group on the surface of GSH-AuNCs would be protonated under acidic conditions, which would affect the complexation between GSH-AuNCs and Cu2+. Under acidic conditions, the protonation of carboxyl group on glutathione reduced its complex with Cu2+. At the same time, the protonation of carboxyl group can also weaken the interaction between GSH-AuNCs and Cu2+. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of GSH-AuNCs was weak under acidic condition (Vedamalai et al., 2014). As shown in Fig. S5b, when Cd2+ was added into the reaction system, the fluorescence was enhanced obviously under neutral and alkaline conditions. At high pH, the surface of GSH-AuNCs can form a compact GSH-Cd complex, which can effectively reduce the non-radiation attenuation pathway through surface passivation, thus greatly enhancing the fluorescence intensity (Huang, Li, Gao, & Wu, 2015). In order to facilitate the actual sample detection, we chose pH = 7 for the further experiments. When Cu2+ and Cd2+ were added into the reaction system, the fluorescence intensity changed rapidly and did not change much within 20 min. The results showed that the reaction was rapid and the stability was good. Therefore, the fluorescence spectra of the reaction system were recorded after the analytes were added into the reaction system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ and Cd2+

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a showed the fluorescence spectrum of the reaction system in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+. Fig. 1b showed the standard curve of (I565/I403)/(I565/I403)0 { (I565 and I403 was the fluorescence intensity of GSH-AuNCs and N-GQDs, respectively. (I565/I403)0 and (I565/I403) were the fluorescence intensity ratios before and after adding Cu2+, respectively.} and the concentration of Cu2+. When Cu2+ was added to the reaction system, the fluorescence intensity of GSH-AuNCs was gradually quenched and the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs changed little. When the concentration of Cu2+ was 8×10−8 mol/L-6×10−6 mol/L, there was a linear relationship between (I565/I403)/(I565/I403)0 and the concentration of Cu2+. The linear regression equation was (I565/I403)/(I565/I403)0 = 0.93–113776.16C(Cu2+) and the linear correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9990. From LOD = 3δ/S (δ is the standard deviation of 11 fluorescence measurements, S is the slope of the standard curve.), the limit of detection was 4.12×10−9 mol/L (9.91 μg/L), which was much lower than the maximum limit of 1.3 mg/L stipulated by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (Nurerk, Kanatharana, & Bunkoed, 2016). In addition, the illustration showed the change of fluorescence color in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. When the concentration of the reaction system was from 0.6 to 6 μmol/L, obvious color change can be seen and the fluorescence color changed from pink to blue. Table S1 displayed the comparison between this method and other methods for measuring Cu2+. Compared with other methods, this method not only did not need expensive instruments, but also had the advantage of simplicity, rapidity and low detection limit. At the same time, it can realize rapid semi-quantitative detection under the irradiation of ultraviolet lamp.

The quenching effect of Cu2+ on the fluorescence of GSH-AuNCs might be caused by charge transfer mechanism. When Cu2+ was added into the reaction system, the Cu2+ was coordinated with the carboxyl group of glutathione to form the GSH-Cu2+ complex. The charge was transferred from the gold nanoclusters to the Cu2+ and the fluorescence of the GSH-AuNCs was quenched. In addition, the fluorescence quenching of GSH-AuNCs was also related to aggregation (Wang et al., 2019).

Fig. 2 a showed the fluorescence spectrum of the reaction system in the presence of different concentrations of Cd2+. Fig. 2b shows the standard curve of (I565/I403)/(I565/I403)0 {(I565/I403)0 and (I565/I403) were the fluorescence intensity ratios before and after adding Cd2+, respectively.} and the concentration of Cd2+. The fluorescence of gold nanoclusters could be enhanced rapidly after Cd2+ was added into the reaction system, while the fluorescence of N-GQDs did not change too much. When the concentration of Cd2+ was 1×10-6 mol/L-4×10−5 mol/L, (I565/I403)/(I565/I403)0 had a linear correlation with the concentration of Cd2+. The linear equation was (I565/I403)/(I565/I403)0 = 1.04 + 17626.12C(Cd2+) and the linear correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9984. From LOD = 3δ/S (δ is the standard deviation of 11 fluorescence measurements, S is the slope of the standard curve.), the limit of detection was 9.43×10−7 mol/L (0.189 mg/L), which was lower than the maximum limit of 1 mg/L stipulated by Commission Regulation (EC) 466/2001(10) (GUTIÉRREZ et al., 2007). The illustration showed the color change under 365 nm UV light when the concentration of Cd2+ was from 6 to 40 μmol/L. The fluorescence color of the reaction system increased gradually. Table S2 displayed the comparison between this method and other methods for measuring Cd2+. Compared with other methods, the detection limit of this method was relatively low. At the same time, it would be used for rapid semi-quantitative detection of Cd2+ under ultraviolet lamp, which had better practicability.

After Cd2+ was added into the reaction system, it could combine with the sulfhydryl group of glutathione to form GSH-Cd complex and form core-shell heterogeneous nanostructure. The formation of this structure passivated the surface of GSH-AuNCs and effectively reduced the non-radiation attenuation pathway. Thus, the fluorescence intensity was greatly enhanced (Huang, Li, Gao, & Wu, 2015; Guo, Zhang, Shao, Wang, Wang, & Jiang, 2014; Mo et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 a, when 0.2 μmol/L Cu2+, 30 μmol/L Cd2+, 0.2 mmol/L Pb2+, 4 mmol/L Zn2+, 4 mmol/L Cr3+, 4 mmol/L Fe3+, 4 mmol/L Fe2+, 4 mmol/L Mg2+, 4 mmol/L K+, 4 mmol/L Mn2+, 4 mmol/L Ba2+, 4 mmol/L Cl−, 4 mmol/L I−, 4 mmol/L SO4 2−, 4 mmol/L CO3 2– or 4 mmol/L F- was added to the reaction system respectively, the fluorescence intensity only decreased in the reaction system with Cu2+ and increased in the reaction system with Cd2+. When Pb2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, K+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Cl−, I−, SO4 2−, CO3 2– or F− was added into the reaction system, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-AuNCs did not change significantly. In another interference experiment, 0.2 μmol/L Cu2+ and 30 μmol/L Cd2+ was mixed with other ions in the reaction system (Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c) and the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system was measured. The results showed that the fluorescent probe had good selectivity for Cu2+ and Cd2+.

We used the relative standard deviation (RSD) to evaluate the precision of the established method. Six scallop samples were analyzed in one day for intra-day reproducibility analysis. Six scallop samples were analyzed within six days for inter-day reproducibility analysis. Table S3 shows that the intra-day precision and inter-day precision of the method were between 1% and 3%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Cu2+ and Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to on-site and determination of Cu2+ and Cd2+, we observed the color changes of the paper-based sensor in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ and Cd2+ under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 4 ). The filter paper was soaked at 4 ℃ for 12 h and dried naturally to make a paper-based fluorescent sensor. Different concentrations of Cu2+ (1 μmol/L, 10 μmol/L, 25 μmol/L, 50 μmol/L and 100 μmol/L) were added to the filter paper fixed by GQDs-AuNCs solution and the color of filter paper gradually changed from purple to blue (Fig. 4a). Different concentrations of Cd2+ (10 μmol/L, 25 μmol/L, 50 μmol/L, 0.1 mmol/L and 1 mmol/L) were added to the filter paper fixed by GQDs-AuNCs solution and the color of the filter paper changed from purple to bright yellow (Fig. 4b). The results demonstrated that the paper-based fluorescent sensor can be used for on-site detection of the Cu2+ and Cd2+ in the concentration range of 1–100 μmol/L and 10–1000 μmol/L, respectively. Moreover, the paper-based fluorescent sensor possessed good stability under ambient conditions and met the requirements for on-site fast detection. Fig. 4c showed the color change of the filter paper when the scallop samples dropped on the paper-based sensor. The color of the filter paper did not change obviously when the scallop samples were added to the filter paper. The color change of filter paper after adding 25 μmol/L Cu2+ or Cd2+ to actual samples were consistent with that of adding 25 μmol/L Cu2+ or Cd2+ only. Based on the above observation, it was easy to visually monitor the concentrations of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in the real samples by combining the paper-based fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallops
ce:section.ce:para: Because the reaction system can quickly identify Cu2+ and Cd2+, the fluorescence spectrum of the reaction system can be measured after adding the samples. Table 1 . showed that Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallops were detected by the constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe. There was no Cu2+ detected in the scallops. The content of Cd2+ in scallops was 9.84×10−7 mol/L (0.198 mg/L). The actual sample test result met the standards of European Communities Commission regulation (EC) 466/2001 (10). The recoveries were between 93% and 104% and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were less than 5%, suggesting a good precision and accuracy of the proposed approach. In order to verify the accuracy of the method, the contents of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallops were measured by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES). The content of Cu2+ in scallops was 2.29×10−9 mol/L (This concentration was lower than the LOD of the method and cannot be detected.). The content of Cd2+ was 1.07×10−6 mol/L. 6×10−7 mol/L Cu2+ and 6×10−6 mol/L Cd2+ were added to the reaction system containing the samples and the contents of Cu2+ and Cd2+ were 6.33×10−7 mol/L and 6.87×10−6 mol/L, respectively. The recoveries were 90.1% and 82.8%, respectively. It was consistent with our results. These results demonstrated that our ratiometric fluorescent method held great potential for Cu2+ and Cd2+ detection in scallop samples.

We have developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for rapid detection of Cu2+ and Cd2+. The fluorescence sensor is a nanomixture of N-GQDs and GSH-AuNCs formed by electrostatic interaction, which can eliminate the influence of environment through internal correction and make the detection results more accurate. When Cu2+ or Cd2+ were added into the reaction system, the fluorescence of GSH-AuNCs would be quenched or enhanced, while the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs remained unchanged as the reference signal. The experimental results show that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor has high selectivity for Cu2+ and Cd2+ and the detection limits are lower than the maximum detection limits of EPA and EC. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, the paper-based sensor can observe obvious color changes in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ or Cd2+. In addition, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor has the advantages of simple preparation, convenient operation and short reaction time and has been successfully applied to the determination of Cu2+ and Cd2+ in scallop samples. The results demonstrate that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor should be the great significance in food security and biological analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by a grant from the National Key Technologies R & D Program of China during the 13th Five-Year Plan Period (No. 2019YFD0901701).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.130964.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
